planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, dry to moderately moist soil in zone 7–10; Mexican Flame (Desert Honeysuckle, Hummingbird Bush) is a native Texas and Mexican drought-tolerant shrub — found naturally on rocky limestone slopes, dry creek beds, and desert margins from the Trans-Pecos to the Hill Country — producing an extraordinary abundance of brilliant orange-red, tubular flowers from midsummer through fall that are irresistible to hummingbirds and are among the most important late-season nectar sources in Texas native plantings.

directions: Plant Anisacanthus quadrifidus in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun; once established it requires little supplemental water — it is highly drought-tolerant and actually performs better without irrigation in most Texas gardens; cut back hard in late winter to encourage compact, bushy growth and maximize summer flowering. Mexican Flame is one of the premier native hummingbird plants of Texas and the northern Mexico borderlands — a deciduous to semi-evergreen shrub that comes alive in the heat of summer and fall when little else is blooming, producing masses of brilliant orange-red tubular flowers on graceful, arching branches just when hummingbirds are fueling up for their fall migration. In zone 7–9 gardens, it dies back to the root crown after hard frost and re-emerges vigorously in spring; in zone 10 it may remain semi-evergreen. Several flower color variants exist: the most common has bright orange-red flowers, but yellow-flowered forms ('Yellow') are also available and equally valued for hummingbird gardens. A superb plant for xeriscape, wildflower, and native plant gardens throughout the Texas Hill Country, Gulf Coast, and adjacent regions — requiring no fertilizer, no regular irrigation once established, and minimal maintenance beyond annual pruning.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Anisacanthus quadrifidus
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — native plants for Texas hummingbirds

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–12; Cardinal's Guard (Firespike) is a large, shade-tolerant tropical shrub from the Caribbean and Central America — naturalized in South Florida and widely grown throughout Gulf Coast gardens — bearing spectacular, tall, brilliant-red, erect flower spikes of small, waxy, tubular flowers in fall and winter that create an extraordinary display in the shade garden and are magnets for hummingbirds during fall migration.

directions: Plant Odontonema tubaeforme in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; water regularly during establishment and through summer; cut back hard in late winter (before new growth emerges) to encourage compact, bushy growth and prevent woodiness; propagate easily from stem cuttings. Cardinal's Guard is one of the most valuable shade-garden flowering shrubs for the Gulf Coast — its spectacular scarlet-red flower spikes in fall and winter create color in shaded spots at a season when most other plants have stopped blooming, and the flowers are an important fuel stop for hummingbirds during fall migration. The plant performs remarkably well in the partial shade of large trees, where most other fall-blooming shrubs fail to flower well; in deep shade, it will grow vegetatively but reduces flowering. In zone 9, it dies back to the ground after hard frost and re-emerges from the roots in spring, re-establishing quickly to bloom again by October–November. In zone 10–12, it grows as a substantial, multi-stemmed shrub year-round. The tall, elegant flower spikes with their waxy, bright-red individual flowers are also excellent cut flowers. An indispensable plant for Gulf Coast butterfly and hummingbird gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Odontonema tubaeforme

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained, moist soil in zone 9–12; Pigeon Berry (Golden Dewdrop, Skyflower) is a fast-growing, ornamental tropical shrub from the Caribbean and tropical Americas — producing cascading sprays of small, lavender-blue to white tubular flowers beloved by butterflies and hummingbirds, followed by clusters of striking golden-yellow, berry-like drupes — widely planted in Gulf Coast gardens but caution: all parts of the plant, especially the berries, are toxic to humans, dogs, and cats.

directions: Plant Duranta erecta in fertile, well-drained, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; prune regularly to control size and shape — it can become a large, arching shrub or small tree without pruning; in zone 9, it dies back to the ground after hard frost but re-emerges in spring. CAUTION: Berries and leaves are toxic — do not plant where young children or pets have unsupervised access; the golden-yellow berries are attractive but cause severe gastroenteritis if ingested. Pigeon Berry is one of the most popular ornamental shrubs in Gulf Coast, tropical, and subtropical gardens for good reason — the cascading clusters of lavender-blue, tubular flowers with a fringed appearance (the 'sky flower') are produced in extraordinary abundance from spring through fall, creating a lavender haze that is irresistible to butterflies (particularly swallowtails, sulphurs, and monarchs) and hummingbirds. Following the flowers, clusters of bright golden-yellow berries persist through winter, providing excellent bird food for mockingbirds, robins, and thrushes — but the berries should not be eaten by people or pets. Several cultivars are available: 'Aurea' with golden-yellow foliage, 'Alba' with white flowers and yellow berries, and 'Golden Edge' with gold-margined leaves. In zone 10–12 it can become a large, sprawling shrub or small tree 15–18 feet tall without regular pruning.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Duranta erecta
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — tropical ornamental shrubs for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–11; Plum Pine (Illawarra Plum, Brown Pine) is a large, handsome Australian rainforest tree — endemic to the rainforests of coastal New South Wales — prized for its small, dark purple to black, edible receptacles (the swollen fruit stalk that surrounds the actual seed) with a rich, complex, slightly piney, sweet-tart flavor increasingly popular in Australian native food cuisine and with commercial potential as a specialty fruit crop.

directions: Plant Podocarpus elatus in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during establishment; mature trees are fairly drought-tolerant; female plants bear the edible receptacles only when a male tree is planted nearby for pollination. Plum Pine is one of the most ecologically and gastronomically interesting of all Australian rainforest trees — a conifer (in the Podocarpaceae family, relatives of pines) whose 'fruit' is technically the swollen, fleshy, purple-black stem (receptacle) that develops beneath the actual seed. The edible receptacles are about 1–2 cm across with dark purple-black skin and rich, soft, slightly jammy flesh with a distinctive, complex flavor that combines sweetness, tartness, and a subtle piney, resinous note unlike any other fruit. Aboriginal Australians used the receptacles as a food source; today Plum Pine is a celebrated ingredient in Australian native food (bush tucker) cuisine — used in jams, sauces, cocktails, and gourmet desserts. Commercial cultivation is expanding in subtropical Australia. In the garden, it is a handsome, slow-growing evergreen tree with dense, narrow, yew-like foliage; it tolerates urban conditions well and makes an excellent shade tree or screen. Dioecious — both male and female trees needed for fruit production.
sources: NSW Environment — Podocarpus elatus ecology and uses
Australian Bush Tucker Manual — Podocarpus elatus
University of New South Wales — native food plants research

subcategory: Eastern Australian ornamental fringed-flower berry tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–11; Blueberry Ash (Fairy Petticoats, Pink Elaeo) is a beautiful, multi-season ornamental tree from eastern Australia — endemic to coastal rainforest margins of Queensland and New South Wales — producing profuse, distinctive, white or pale-pink, delicately fringed bell-shaped flowers in spring, followed by year-round clusters of brilliant blue-violet to cobalt-blue, blueberry-like drupes much loved by birds.

directions: Plant Elaeocarpus reticulatus in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; water regularly during establishment and in dry periods; mulch to maintain soil moisture and acidity; grows well in coastal gardens with some protection from strong wind. Blueberry Ash is one of the most ornamentally multi-season of all Australian native trees — providing year-round interest through its attractive, elliptical, fine-toothed leaves; the exquisite spring flowers (white or blush-pink, tiny fringed bells hanging in clusters, earning the nickname 'Fairy Petticoats'); and the persistent, brilliant cobalt-blue to blue-violet, blueberry-sized drupes that can be present on the tree year-round and are avidly consumed by parrots, doves, and other birds. The blue fruits are technically edible for humans but very astringent and are not eaten except in famine conditions; birds have no such reservation. Elaeocarpus reticulatus is a small to medium rainforest tree (20–40 feet in the wild) that adapts well to cultivation in garden situations, particularly in the humid, subtropical Gulf Coast climate. It makes an outstanding specimen, street tree, or privacy screen for zone 9–11 gardens; the year-round blue berries make it perhaps the most effective bird-attracting tree available for subtropical plantings.
sources: Australian Native Plants Society — Elaeocarpus reticulatus

subcategory: South African rose-scented aromatic culinary pelargonium

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in zone 9–11; Rose Geranium is a South African shrubby pelargonium — the primary commercial source of geranium essential oil (used extensively in perfumery, aromatherapy, and skin care) — with deeply lobed, velvety, fragrant leaves that smell intensely of fresh roses, used to flavor jellies, cakes, lemonade, and tea, and producing small pink flowers with darker veining throughout the warm season.

directions: Root Rose Geranium stem cuttings in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; avoid overwatering — the plant prefers to dry out between waterings and is highly susceptible to root rot in wet conditions; prune regularly to maintain compact, bushy shape and encourage continuous fresh, fragrant foliage; fertilize lightly. Rose Geranium is the primary commercial source of geranium essential oil — steam-distilled from the leaves and stems — and is one of the most important fragrance crops in the world, with major production in Egypt, China, South Africa, and Réunion Island. The oil is used extensively in fine perfumery (often blending as a rose substitute or modifier), aromatherapy, and high-end skin care products. In the culinary garden, the intensely rose-fragrant leaves are used fresh: layered in sugar (rose geranium sugar) to transfer their scent; pressed into the bottom of cake tins before baking (rose geranium pound cake); infused in cream for ice cream; floated in lemonade and iced tea; and used to scent herb vinegars and syrups. The flavor is a delicate rose with a slight leafy, green note — distinctive and sophisticated. A well-drained, hot, sunny position suits Rose Geranium perfectly in Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens; it grows vigorously in the long warm season and tolerates light frost with some protection.
sources: South African Essential Oil Association — Pelargonium graveolens production
Herb Society of America — rose geranium cultivation guide
